goals.caution: In reactive tendinopathy progress load gradually and keep pain within acceptable limits (monitor 24 h response)
Control load and squat depth in symptomatic knee OA; avoid painful end-range flexion during flares
reduces patellofemoral compressive load, but keep step depth moderate early to avoid provoking anterior knee pain
perform in a controlled, obstacle-free space with a rail or spotter for at-risk individuals
high lumbar compressive load in the bent-over position — maintain a neutral spine and progress load gradually
substantial isometric lumbar load in the hinged position — keep the spine neutral and build load progressively
keep the lumbar spine neutral and avoid end-range flexion during an acute pain flare
introduce heavy-slow calf loading before explosive/energy-storage reps in reactive or irritable tendinopathy
Ease off if end-range lumbar extension provokes symptoms (e.g., stenosis or spondylolysis).
Introduce with a short lever and low volume; the adductors are highly loaded and prone to DOMS/strain early on.
Keep squat depth within the pain-free range in anterior knee pain.
high landing forces; establish an eccentric strength base and progress drop height gradually
loaded end-range shoulder extension can provoke anterior shoulder pain; limit depth if symptomatic
avoid aggressive end-range stretch in insertional Achilles tendinopathy
introduce gradually and reduce step height if it reproduces anterior knee pain
monitor for a pain flare and scale volume in an irritable knee
keep the knee tracking over the foot and lower step height if it provokes anterior knee pain
thumbs-down internal-rotation position can provoke subacromial impingement; switch to a full-can (thumbs-up) version if painful
progress depth gradually and limit painful ranges during an anterior knee pain flare
manage squat depth and load to keep patellofemoral loading within a tolerable, non-provocative knee-pain range
load heavy but progress gradually, keeping tendon symptoms within an acceptable range
progress load gradually in reactive tendinopathy
progress load gradually; monitor for acceptable, non-escalating tendon pain
reintroduce and load gradually after acute strain; keep spine neutral
long-lever anti-extension load can aggravate an acute flare; bend the knees or shorten the lever early on
high lumbar shear/load in the hinged position; use light load and a strictly neutral spine
keep a neutral spine and hinge from the hips; ballistic loading can provoke an acute low-back flare if the lumbar spine flexes under speed
keep loads within a pain-free arc; progress gradually in an irritable shoulder
reserve for late-stage return-to-sport once single-leg strength and balance are restored; high impact loads an unstable ankle
open-chain terminal knee extension raises patellofemoral joint stress; limit range (avoid the final ~0-30 degrees) during irritable phases
Introduce gradually after the acute phase; the high eccentric load provokes marked DOMS and can aggravate a fresh strain if progressed too fast
Introduce gradually after the acute phase; the high eccentric load provokes marked DOMS and can aggravate a fresh strain if progressed too fast
Keep motion passive and pain-free in the acute/irritable phase; it is an early-stage ROM drill, not a loading exercise
ease into external rotation and flexion range; avoid forcing an irritable or arthritic hip into end range
introduce Nordic eccentrics at reduced range/load early in rehab to avoid over-lengthening the healing muscle
technically demanding; groove the catch and progress load gradually to protect wrists and shoulders
avoid pushing into painful end-range extension during an acute flare; start with small range against gravity
the 90 abduction position loads supraspinatus heavily; begin with light load in an irritable cuff and progress gradually
abducted arm position may provoke impingement symptoms; reduce abduction angle if painful
keep the motion at the hip and avoid arching the lumbar spine to lift the leg higher
indicated only when symptoms centralize with extension; stop if repeated extension peripheralizes leg symptoms
flexion-biased drill; ease off if it provokes leg/nerve symptoms in a disc-related presentation
glide, don't stretch, to first onset of symptoms; back off if it flares nerve pain
keep the front shin more vertical and stay in a pain-free depth
introduce after the acute phase and progress hinge depth/load gradually
maintain a neutral spine; avoid loaded lumbar rounding at end range
progress load gradually in reactive tendinopathy
limit to the pain-free inner range; loaded end-range open-chain extension can increase patellofemoral stress
high patellofemoral joint load; limit depth and progress range gradually during anterior knee pain
Repeated loaded lumbar flexion with strong hip-flexor drive raises spinal load; a poor choice during low-back-pain flares or for those with flexion-intolerant backs.
Progress load and range gradually in reactive tendinopathy and monitor 24-hour tendon response.
Progress load and heel-drop range gradually in reactive tendinopathy and monitor 24-hour tendon response.
Lower step height and limit knee flexion if the step-up reproduces anterior knee pain.
avoid aggressive end-range stretch into pain during an acute or early-healing strain
extension-biased drill; discontinue if it increases or peripheralizes leg symptoms, and avoid in extension-sensitive presentations
control knee valgus and keep pain-free; regress step height if anterior knee pain is provoked
keep the knee fully extended and stay pain-free
high lumbar compressive load; avoid during an acute low back pain flare
progress length gently early after strain; avoid aggressive end-range pull in the acute/painful phase
avoid heavy eccentric loading during the acute painful phase; progress range and load gradually
progress eccentric load gradually; expect transient soreness and avoid pushing into sharp pain
keep squat depth in a pain-free range; deep knee flexion increases patellofemoral joint compression
keep the knees in a pain-free flexion range; deeper angles raise patellofemoral compression
hold at a pain-free knee-flexion depth; deeper angles increase patellofemoral compression
keep load light and stay below a painful arc in a reactive/irritable cuff
load the extensors progressively and keep within pain-tolerable range in reactive lateral epicondylalgia
